content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

September 13, 2017

Sue Gallagher
City Attorney
City of Santa Rosa
100 Santa Rosa Avenue, Room 8
Santa Rosa, CA 95404

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-17-204

Dear Ms. Gallagher:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Santa Rosa Vice Mayor Jack
Tibbetts regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act,
and we offer no opinion on the applicability of other conflict of interest laws, such as Section 1090
or the Public Contract Code. We are also not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby
(1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate.

QUESTION

Considering that in his private capacity the Vice Mayor is the paid Executive Director of St.
Vincent de Paul Sonoma County, a spiritually based nonprofit organization seeking to alleviate
hunger and poverty in Sonoma County, does the Act prohibit the Vice Mayor from taking part in
governmental decisions relating to:

• The City's general policies to address local homelessness?

• Potential new City initiatives or policies relating to homelessness?

• Whether to renew the City's monthly Declaration of Local Homeless Emergency?

• The City's potential entrance into, or extension of, contracts with Catholic Charities or
alternative service providers (other than St. Vincent) for operation of the City's emergency
shelter or Homeless Outreach Services Team ("HOST") program, or for funding for other
services for the homeless?

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-204
Page No. 2

• Whether to expand, modify, or terminate the City's Community Homeless Assistance Program ("CHAP")?

• Whether the City will participate with other public entities in regional efforts to address homelessness?

• Whether to continue or expand the City's pilot program for the clearing of encampments?

CONCLUSION

No. The Act does not prohibit the Vice Mayor from taking part in these decisions so long as each decision would not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of his financial interests.

FACTS

You are the City Attorney for the City of Santa Rosa and the authorized representative of Santa Rosa Vice Mayor Jack Tibbetts. In his role as Vice Mayor, Mr. Tibbetts is a voting member of the Santa Rosa City Council.

In his private capacity, the Vice Mayor is employed by St. Vincent de Paul Sonoma County ("St. Vincent") as its paid Executive Director. Headquartered in the nearby City of Rohnert Park, St. Vincent identifies itself as "a non-profit spiritually-based organization of individuals seeking to alleviate poverty and hunger in Sonoma County." St. Vincent operates a dining room located in Santa Rosa, which is open 365 days a year and serves free hot meals to anyone who enters. St. Vincent also operates a thrift store and job-training program in Rohnert Park, and has an associated volunteer team that travels throughout the County, providing home visits, social service referrals, and a food pantry.

St Vincent currently has no contracts or financial dealings with the City. St. Vincent is not a participant in any of the City's homeless housing programs or initiatives, nor has it been in the past as far your office is aware. It is your office's understanding St. Vincent also has no contracts or financial dealings with any other public entity. Although St. Vincent has recently been approached regarding potential participation in certain City and County programs, it has declined to participate in those programs.

For its part, the City has a strong and expanding program to address local homelessness. For many years, the City has owned and operated a local emergency shelter. In the last several years, the City has undertaken a number of additional programs and initiatives to address homelessness within the City, including:

• Participation in and funding of the City's HOST program, which provides front-line services to homeless individuals and strives to link them into shelter and appropriate support services;

File No. A-17-204
Page No. 3

• Adoption of a Declaration of Local Homeless Emergency under Sections 8558 and 8630 to 8634, and the adoption of a Declaration of Shelter Crisis under Sections 8698 to 8698.2, both of which give the City some flexibility to waive or adjust City land use and other regulations to address homelessness;

• Adoption of the CHAP, which allows private organizations to provide, on their own properties, temporary emergency shelter, safe parking, safe camping, storage facilities, and similar assistance to those experiencing homelessness;

• Provision of limited funding for CHAP participants;

• Participation or cooperation in regional efforts with other public entities and nonprofit homeless service providers, including Continuum of Care, the Santa Rosa Homeless Collective, and others;

• Providing financial support to allow for expansion and transition of the City's homeless shelter to a housing-first model; and

• Adoption of a pilot program for clearing of unauthorized encampments, including provision of shelter and services for occupants, temporary storage of belongings, veterinary services for pets, and site clean-up.

The City contracts with Catholic Charities of the Diocese of Santa Rosa to operate the City's emergency shelter and lead the HOST program. Although other local nonprofit organizations participate in the City's homeless services efforts through both the HOST and CHAP programs, St. Vincent has not participated, nor sought participation, in any of the City's programs or initiatives. According to the Vice Mayor, St. Vincent has no plans to participate in the City's programs relating to homelessness in the future.

ANALYSIS

The Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, or attempting use his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. (Section 87100.) An official has a "financial interest" in a decision, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the official's interests identified by the Act. (Section 87103.) Of the interests identified by the Act, the decisions at issue and the facts presented implicate the following of the Vice Mayor's interests:

• Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial lending institution made in the regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status, aggregating $500 or more in value promised or provided to, or received by, the official within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).) The Vice Mayor has a source-of-income interest in St. Vincent because the Vice Mayor is employed as its paid Executive Director.

• An interest in his or her personal finances and those of immediate family members. An official always has an interest in his or her personal finances.

File No. A-17-204
Page No. 4

Foreseeability and Materiality

A decision's financial effect on an official's interest is presumed to be reasonably
foreseeable if the interest is a named party in, or the subject of, the decision. (Regulation 18701(a).)
An interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval,
denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the interest.
(Ibid.) If the interest is not a named party in or the subject of the decision, the decision's effect on
the interest is reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than
hypothetical or theoretical. (Regulation 18701(b).)

Neither St. Vincent nor the Vice Mayor's personal finances is a named party in or the
subject of the decisions at issue based on the facts presented.² Therefore, the financial effect of each
of those decisions on one or more of the Vice Mayor's interests is reasonably foreseeable if it can
be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical.

St. Vincent

Regulation 18702.3 sets forth the Act's materiality standards applicable to a decision's
effect on an official's interest in a source of income. Paragraph (3) of subdivision (a) of that
regulation provides in pertinent part that the decision's effect is material if the source of income is a
nonprofit organization that will receive a measurable financial benefit or loss due to the decision.

St. Vincent currently has no contracts or financial dealings with the City. And it is your
office's understanding that St. Vincent has no contracts or financial dealings with any other public
entity. St. Vincent has not participated, nor sought participation, in any of the City's programs or
initiatives, and has no plans to participate in City's programs relating to homelessness in the future
according to the Vice Mayor.

One may argue that the City's policies and programs relating to homelessness subsidize
other organizations with similar goals as St. Vincent, and thereby increase the competition that St.
Vincent faces to alleviate hunger and poverty within Sonoma County. However, there is no
indication that additional competition to alleviate hunger and poverty within the County would have
any ascertainable or discernable financial effect on St. Vincent in the foreseeable future.

Therefore, examining each of the decisions at issue, there is no indication that any of those
decisions would result in a measurable financial benefit or loss to St. Vincent based on the facts
presented.

Nexus Test

Subdivision (c) of Regulation 18702.3 sets forth the Nexus Test, which provides that any
reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a source of income to an official is deemed material if the
official receives or is promised the income to achieve a goal or purpose which would be achieved,
defeated, aided, or hindered by the decision.

² However, if St. Vincent becomes a named party or the subject of one of those decisions, the effect of that
decision will be deemed reasonably foreseeable pursuant to Regulation 18701(a).

File No. A-17-204
Page No. 5

Consistent with the conclusion above that the decisions at issue would not result in a
measurable financial benefit or loss to St. Vincent, the facts presented also provide no indication
that any of those decisions would have any financial effect on St. Vincent. Therefore, the Nexus
Test does not apply.

Personal Finances

Regulation 18702.5(a) provides that a decision's financial effect on an official's personal
finances is material if the official will receive a measurable financial benefit or loss from the
decision. The facts presented provide no indication that any of the decisions at issue will result in a
measurable financial benefit or loss to the Vice Mayor's personal finances.

Thus, the Act does not prohibit the Vice Mayor from taking part in the decisions at issue
because those decisions would not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the
Vice Mayor's financial interests based on the facts presented.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Jack Woodside
General Counsel

[signature]

By: Matthew F. Christy
Counsel, Legal Division

MFC:jgl

sections.question:
Considering that in his private capacity the Vice Mayor is the paid Executive Director of St.

alternative service providers (other than St. Vincent) for operation of the City's emergency 
shelter or Homeless Outreach Services Team ("HOST") program, or for funding for other 
services for the homeless?• 
Whether to expand, modify, or tenninate the City's Community Homeless Assistance Program 
( .. CHAP")? 
• 
Whether the City will participate with other public entities in regional efforts to address 
homelessness? 
• 
Whether to continue or expand the City's pilot program for the clearing of encampments?
sections.conclusion:
No. The Act does not prohibit the Vice Mayor from taking part in these decisions so long as 
each decision would not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of 
his financial interests.

interest in St. Vincent because the Vice Mayor is employed as its paid Executive Director.

(Ibid.) If the interest is not a named party in or the subject of the decision, the decision's effect on

Neither St. Vincent nor the Vice Mayor's personal finances is a named party in or the

be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. 
St. Vincent

effect on an official's interest in a source of income. Paragraph (3) of subdivision (a) of that 
regulation provides in pertinent part that the decision's effect is material if the source of income is a

St. Vincent currently has no contracts or financial dealings with the City. And it is your

Nexus Test

2 However, if St. Vincent becomes a named party or the subject of one of those decisions, the effect of that

Personal Finances 
Regulation J 8702.S(a) provides that a decision's financial effect on an official's personal

homelessness?

CONCLUSION: No. The Act does not prohibit the Vice Mayor from taking part in these decisions so long as

his financial interests.
